speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape four, interview with Helena Snidman. I was asking you before if you encountered anti-Semitism when you first arrived in Canada, and when you had your first jobs.

speaker: interviewee
text: First job was at a Jewish factory-- a Jewish factory. Mr. Clarfeld. He used to produce shirts, and I was folding them. I got that job. And then I didn't go anywhere-- no theater, no opera, no ballet. So I had no chance of encountering anything personal. Except that I bought that book by Professor Abella-- And I read the history about Canada, how they behaved during the war, which was terrible. I have the book in my library.

speaker: interviewee
text: But personal, me? Nobody ever-- ever threw me in my face, you Jew, that's why I don't like you, or I don't want you. I didn't. But in the store now, I hear very often people whispering one to another, oh, that's a Jewish store, let's go. Now, this is the store that you own today?

speaker: interviewer
text: So you've heard people say things like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, between themself. They-- they can say in Russian, they can say in German, they can say in Polish, and I understand. And they don't know that I understand. Even a lot o' colored people. They don't like Jews. They talk between themself.

speaker: interviewer
text: And a lot of them understand other languages, too. Because before they come to Canada, they stationed in Europe, you know? A lot in Italy and Austria, like we used to do during the war-- In Lagos. How did you find your adjustments when you moved here and find your job?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was very hard. We worked very, very hard. After being in Canada six years, we [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] opened up a variety store. Again, variety, because that was the easiest way with my English to communicate. Like selling cigarettes, like pop, like chocolate bars, like peanuts. That's what we were selling. And we were working there for almost 10 years, me and my husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the children were living upstairs from the store. It was on Dunder Street. Dunder and [NON-ENGLISH]. Then we bought a bungalow.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was your third daughter born?

speaker: interviewer
text: When was your third daughter born?

speaker: interviewee
text: My third daughter was born in 1945. This is Lola, what she came in. That's my daughter. In-- in the 8th of February, 1955.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was she born when you were working at the variety store already?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. We had a variety store. And the children were living above the store, and me and my husband were working downstairs. And we saved up money. We bought a bungalow on [NON-ENGLISH] on [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH] number eight. We bought a bungalow. And because I wanted my children to be between better class, you know. I was used to from home. And on Dunder was not-- it was a good section for business, but wasn't a good section for me to raise my children.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find that your experiences during the war affected the way that you raised your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much so. I raise them with respect, the way I was raised in my home. They know my whole life story, which is to write three books. If I would want to say everything, you have to sit with me here a month. Everything in details. And my children carry on their home. And they children are raised exactly the way I raised my children. Respect for God, and respect for people. [CRYING] [SOBBING] That's the motto. Respect. And they respect me for that. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] They are good citizen. [SOBBING] They have the schooling here. We worked very hard. We gave them ballet lessons, and we gave them piano. And they went to school. So they didn't go to university, because they had to go to work. So they finish [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that typing, you know, shorthand type of schooling. And now, two of my daughters are working in a store. Lola, the youngest, is married for a dentist. So she works with her husband, too. Exactly the way I used to carry on my life with my husband-- side-by-side, to help. And she helps out her husband in the office, too. She raise three children. And they go to school. And she gives them the same thing, like I try, even not be able to afford that. She gives them piano, and she gives them Hebrew. Everyone had bar mitzvahs, and bat mitzvahs, and Hebrew. And I try to speak to them Yiddish. The way I was brought up. The way I brought up my children. And the way-- the way they bringing up their children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this is my two granddaughters. And my son-in-law. OK, we're just getting close to the end. I wanted to ask you what your-- your final thoughts are. And If you have--

speaker: interviewer
text: Just hold the question up til I get through her, OK? Just wait til I get through her?

speaker: interviewee
text: My final thought about Canada-- [SOBBING] it's a good country. And I thank God that I chose Canada to be my home and to raise my children. And I'm very happy to be a decent and productive citizen and to do my utmost. I do charity, what I can. I never say no to no charity. If I can't afford it a lot, I do little. But never, never-- anybody, I don't say no. I give a donation to sick children. I give a donation to the hurt. I give a donation to [INAUDIBLE] Center-- anywhere they send me a note. We belong to a shul. We are decent citizen. So is my children. And I'm very happy to be Canadian.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you very much for speaking with us today.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you for giving your time. I'm sorry, I didn't mean to disturb you. But it's a very sensitive time for me--

speaker: interviewee
text: You wanted me to introduce the family? Yeah-- to my right is my daughter Rena and her husband, [SOBBING] Harold. It's Rachel, Joshua, Felicia, Mark Kochman and Lola, my daughter. My grandson, David, and my grandson, Toben. Sitting is Dahlia, Danielle, Rubin, Ilana, Shana.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my daughter Rena and her husband, Harold Keen. They have four children, Dahlia, Shana, David, and Danielle. Next to me is my daughter, Rachel, her daughter, Felicia, and her son, Joshua. At my left is my daughter, Lola, her husband-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Mark, [LAUGHTER] [INTERPOSING VOICES] and her son, Toben and Rubin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now? I am at the left. And this is my girlfriend Bronka Hlivner In 1947, in Poland, at our hometown, Warsaw.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me in Poland, 1947, in our hometown in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: How old was I? I forgot. '47, from '17. So that means I was 30 years old. In the middle, the stand up, the young lady-- this is my oldest daughter, Rena, in kindergarten in Poland in 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, where is your daughter, Rena?

speaker: interviewee
text: She is here, standing up with the ribbon in her hair.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is this group of children?

speaker: interviewee
text: They are kindergarten. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] And the bottom line, the third one from the right in the middle is my oldest daughter, Rena, in kindergarten. [SOBBING] The picture was taken in Dubno. This is Gideon Snidman, my father-in-law, [SOBBING] my husband's father. This picture is me and my husband in Dubno when we came back from Russia in 1947.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Russia. And Rena was there. She is in the middle. Rena was there nine or 10-month-old. I don't remember exactly. But it was made in Russia, in Dubno.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken when my husband got his citizen paper after six years in Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the name of your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Michael Snidman. This picture was taken just before we left for Israel, in Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's me. That's me, Helen Snidman.

speaker: interviewee
text: Did I smoke a lot? No, I didn't smoke a lot. I just show off. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Now? At left is my daughter, Rena. And this is the picture which was made for the passport to go to Israel. The middle is Rachel. And the last one is a Canadian picture, which is from Lola. And she is three years old here.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was 1961. And that's Lola's sixth birthday. It's our first portrait in Canada. From left is Rena. Next to her is Lola, standing behind is my husband, Michael Snidman. Sitting next to Lola is me, Helen Snidman. And Rachel, the last one. This is me, Helen, and Michael, my husband, at Joshua's bar mitzvah, which is three years ago. 1992. That's the last picture of my husband at Shana's birthday. From the left in the front, next to Michael, is Ilana. Next to Michael is Shana.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then is-- who is that, David? I don't see that well. No, no, that's not David. Who is that? I think Felicia. It's Felicia, behind. Felicia, is the mother Rachel. And then next behind Shana is Rubin. And then is-- who is that? I can't see without the glasses. That's your whole family.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my whole family at Shana's birthday, and the last picture my husband alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Start from the beginning, just say--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Shana's birthday party, and that's the last picture my husband alive. In--
